name: Valentinian Gnosis
description: Valentinian Gnosis represents one of the most influential and elaborate systems of Gnostic thought in the 2nd century CE. Founded by Valentinus, an Egyptian theologian who taught in Rome, it offered a complex cosmology and soteriology distinct from nascent orthodox Christianity. Valentinianism posited a divine pleroma (fullness) of aeons (divine emanations), disrupted by Sophia's (Wisdom) desire to know the unknowable Father. This disruption led to the creation of the Demiurge, an ignorant and flawed creator god responsible for the material world. Humans, according to Valentinianism, possess a spark of the divine (the pneuma or spirit) and can achieve salvation (gnosis) through secret knowledge and rituals, escaping the Demiurge's control and returning to the pleroma. Valentinian texts, discovered at Nag Hammadi, offer insights into their beliefs, including reinterpretations of biblical figures and stories. Mainstream scholarship views Valentinianism as a heterodox interpretation of Christianity, influenced by Platonic philosophy and Near Eastern mythology. Critics within early Christianity, such as Irenaeus and Tertullian, condemned it as heresy, distorting the true message of Jesus. Modern scholars debate the extent to which Valentinianism was a unified system or a diverse collection of related ideas.
subtitle: Complex Gnostic system of divine emanations.

claims.claim: The material world is the creation of an ignorant Demiurge.
claims.evidence: Valentinian texts describe the Demiurge as flawed and unaware of the true God.
claims.criticism: Mainstream Christianity asserts that God is the benevolent creator of the world, not a flawed Demiurge.

claims.claim: Humans possess a divine spark (pneuma) that can be awakened through gnosis.
claims.evidence: Valentinian texts emphasize the importance of inner knowledge and spiritual enlightenment for salvation.
claims.criticism: Mainstream Christianity teaches that salvation is achieved through faith in Jesus Christ and adherence to the Church's sacraments, not through secret knowledge.

claims.claim: The pleroma is a realm of divine aeons emanating from the ultimate God.
claims.evidence: Valentinian cosmological myths detail the complex relationships and interactions within the pleroma.
claims.criticism: Mainstream Christianity rejects the concept of a pleroma and affirms the singularity of God.

timeline.event: Flourishing of Valentinian schools and texts
Decline of Valentinianism due to Christian persecution and internal divisions
Discovery of the Nag Hammadi Library, containing Valentinian texts

sources.author: Kurt Rudolph
sources.work: Gnosis: The Nature and History of Gnosticism
sources.title: Kurt Rudolph
